topic: JetBrains Air Review 2026: Multi-Agent Development Environment from JetBrains

key_points: JetBrains Air Review 2026: Multi-Agent IDE vs Cursor and Copilot
Air best for greenfield development and large refactoring; Cursor still better for single-file deep work
Multiple agents touching related files can cause merge conflicts — Air handles this better than manual tool switching but not friction-free
Recommended: use Air for big-picture changes, keep Cursor for precision production work
JetBrains Air: The Future of Multi-Agent Coding, or Just More AI Noise?
Skeptical take: orchestration layer sometimes introduces latency that offsets parallelism gains in complex codebases
ACP (Agent Client Protocol) is a key differentiator — like LSP but for AI agents
Air is built on abandoned Fleet IDE codebase, raising concerns about long-term commitment
JetBrains Air: agentic IDE built on abandoned Fleet
The Register notes JetBrains previously abandoned Fleet IDE and now repurposed its codebase for Air
Public preview launched March 2026, macOS only initially
Supports Codex, Claude Agent, Gemini CLI, and JetBrains Junie out of the box
JetBrains Launches Air Preview for Developers Managing Multiple AI Agents
Air focuses on agent orchestration rather than replacing the code editor
Supports Docker containers and Git worktrees for sandboxing and concurrent agent isolation
BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) support for Anthropic, OpenAI, Google API keys
JetBrains Launches Air and Junie CLI to Blend Traditional IDE with AI Agents
Junie CLI launched alongside Air — allows running Junie agent from the command line
Air integrates MCP (Model Context Protocol) for connecting external tools and data sources
JetBrains positions Air as complementary to existing IDEs, not a replacement

stats.fact: JetBrains Air launched as public preview in March 2026, free for macOS; Windows and Linux versions in development
Air is free for JetBrains AI subscribers (AI Pro or AI Ultimate); BYOK option available for users with Anthropic, OpenAI, or Google API keys
Junie (JetBrains' own AI agent) starts at $10/month for individuals and up to $60/month for enterprise
11% of developers worldwide use JetBrains AI Assistant and/or Junie (9% AI Assistant, 5% Junie)
stats.source: JetBrains Research Blog, April 2026 — Which AI Coding Tools Do Developers Actually Use at Work?

stats.fact: JetBrains tools are used by teams in 88 of the Fortune Global Top 100 companies
JetBrains AI adoption increased in every quarter of 2025, with the largest quarter-over-quarter gain of +101.33% recorded in Q2
90% of developers regularly used at least one AI tool at work by January 2026
Air supports ACP (Agent Client Protocol) — an open standard for AI coding agents similar to LSP for language servers
stats.source: JetBrains ACP Agent Registry Blog, January 2026

stats.fact: Practical Air workflow example: migrate a legacy module to a new framework in one Git worktree with Claude Agent while Junie generates test suite in a second worktree — both monitored from a single Air dashboard
stats.source: JetBrains Air official documentation and dev.to community article, 2026

stats.fact: Air is built on the codebase of JetBrains Fleet, the collaborative IDE that JetBrains discontinued

angles: Air isn't another AI code editor — it's an orchestration layer that sits above your existing IDE, letting you run Codex, Claude, Gemini, and Junie simultaneously on different tasks
The BYOK angle: developers who already pay for Claude or GPT-4 API can use Air at zero additional cost by plugging in their existing API keys
Built on Fleet's bones: JetBrains repurposed its abandoned Fleet IDE codebase for Air — a controversial but pragmatic move
ACP as the new LSP: the Agent Client Protocol is JetBrains' bid to become the standard connector between IDEs and AI agents, similar to how LSP unified language server integration

Parallelism paradox: Air genuinely accelerates large refactors by running multiple agents concurrently, but three agents touching related files creates merge conflicts that offset gains in complex codebases
The complement play: JetBrains explicitly positions Air as complementary to IntelliJ/PyCharm/WebStorm, not a replacement — unlike Cursor's VS Code fork approach
Enterprise angle: 88 of the Fortune 100 already use JetBrains tools; Air gives their dev teams a sanctioned path to multi-agent AI without switching IDEs

H2: Key Features: Multi-Agent Orchestration with Codex, Claude, Gemini, and Junie
H2: How Air Works: Docker Containers, Git Worktrees, and Parallel Agent Isolation
H2: JetBrains Air Pricing: Free with BYOK, or Bundled with AI Subscriptions

H2: Real-World Use Cases: When Air Shines (and When It Doesn't)
